topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an irritating, high-pitched whine that seemed to vibrate directly behind my eyes. I stared at the printed accusation, the ink blurring into meaningless shapes. My hands, usually steady enough to navigate complex equations, felt clammy and unresponsive as I tried to grasp the words. Every sentence seemed to swim, a current pulling me under. I kept blinking, hoping the sharp, sterile light would snap me back to something solid, but the room remained a dizzying vortex of beige walls and silent judgment. A low, tight knot formed in my chest, making each breath shallow and ragged. I could only manage a faint, dry sound when I tried to speak, the sound catching in my throat like broken glass.

[story 2]

The air in the small conference room felt thick, suffocating, like breathing underwater. I kept running a hand over my forehead, feeling the damp stickiness of sweat against my skin. The faces around the table were sharp, unreadable masks of accusation. I tried to focus on the projector screen, but the lines of text seemed to shift, rearranging themselves into a chaotic pattern that refused to resolve. My thoughts were fragmented, like scattered pieces of glass refusing to fit back together. I shifted in my seat, the cheap plastic chair protesting with a squeak that sounded deafening in the strained silence. I looked down at my notes, but the familiar diagrams swam, offering no anchor in the rising tide of uncertainty washing over me.

[story 3]

It was the silence after the accusation that was the worst. A vast, echoing emptiness where logic used to reside. I stood by the window, watching the rain streak down the glass, each drop distorting the city lights into smeared watercolors. My feet felt oddly disconnected from the floor; the ground seemed unsteady, as if the entire structure of reality was tilting on its axis. I tried to recall the assignment, the specific paragraph I thought I had written, but the memory was elusive, a phantom limb I couldn't quite reach. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I took a slow, deliberate breath, trying to anchor myself to the present moment, but the present felt slippery, intangible, like trying to hold smoke.
